items.title: Okendo Reviews Supply Chain Attack
items.description: On May 14, 2026, a supply chain attack was discovered targeting the Okendo Reviews widget, a customer review platform used by over 18,000 brands. The threat actor injected malicious JavaScript code into the legitimate widget, which is deployed on high-traffic e-commerce pages including storefronts and product pages. The compromised JavaScript acted as a staged loader, using obfuscation, localStora
items.ai_summary: The Okendo Reviews supply chain attack involves the injection of malicious JavaScript code into the legitimate Okendo Reviews widget, which is used by over 18,000 brands. The compromised JavaScript acts as a staged loader, utilizing obfuscation and local storage to evade detection. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive customer data, including personally identifiable information and payment details, ultimately affecting the reputation and financial stability of the affected brands. To remediate this threat, defenders should immediately remove the compromised Okendo Reviews widget from their e-commerce pages and replace it with a verified, clean version, while also monitoring for suspicious activity and implementing additional security measures, such as web application firewalls and regular security audits. Furthermore, defenders should ensure that all dependencies and third-party libraries are up-to-date and patched against known vulnerabilities.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1190| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.007| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1027| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Inside Banana RAT: From Build Server to Banking Fraud
items.description: An MDR investigation successfully mapped the complete operational infrastructure of Banana RAT, a Brazilian banking trojan operated by threat cluster SHADOW-WATER-063. The investigation uncovered both server-side and client-side components, revealing a sophisticated FastAPI-based polymorphic payload generation system that produces hash-unique builds to evade detection. The malware employs layered
items.ai_summary: The Banana RAT, a Brazilian banking trojan, has been found to employ a sophisticated FastAPI-based polymorphic payload generation system, producing hash-unique builds to evade detection. This malware is operated by the threat cluster SHADOW-WATER-063 and has been linked to banking fraud. The RAT uses layered defenses and has both server-side and client-side components, making it a complex threat to defend against. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust network monitoring and detection capabilities, focusing on identifying unusual patterns of traffic to and from the affected systems, and ensure that all security software is up-to-date, with specific attention to ensuring that any FastAPI applications are secured and monitored. Additionally, implementing a Web Application Firewall (WAF) can help mitigate the risk of exploitation of public-facing applications, such as those targeted by T1190.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1562| C[Impair Defenses]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Latest PyPi Compromise
items.description: A supply chain attack targeting the Microsoft DurableTask Python client compromised versions 1.4.1, 1.4.2, and 1.4.3 on PyPi. The threat actor gained access through a compromised GitHub account previously linked to attacks, using stolen credentials to dump GitHub secrets containing PyPi tokens. The evolved payload targets Linux systems, stealing credentials from AWS, Azure, GCP, Kubernetes, Vault,
items.ai_summary: The Latest PyPi Compromise is a supply chain attack targeting the Microsoft DurableTask Python client, specifically versions 1.4.1, 1.4.2, and 1.4.3. The threat actor gained access through a compromised GitHub account, utilizing stolen credentials to dump GitHub secrets containing PyPi tokens, which is an example of T1078 (Valid Accounts) and T1195 (Supply Chain Compromise). This evolved payload targets Linux systems, stealing credentials from cloud providers such as AWS, Azure, GCP, as well as Kubernetes and Vault, resulting in significant real-world impact. To remediate, defenders should immediately update to a version outside the affected range, such as version 1.4.4 or later, and review their GitHub account security, ensuring two-factor authentication is enabled and monitoring for suspicious activity. Additionally, rotating credentials for affected cloud services and secrets management systems is crucial to prevent further exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1078| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1195| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1078| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1195| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Analysis of Gamaredon campaign targeting Ukraine weaponizing CVE-2025-8088
items.description: A campaign exploiting the WinRAR path-traversal vulnerability CVE-2025-8088 has been actively targeting Ukraine since February 2026, with ongoing activity through June 2026. The operation uses Ukrainian military and conscription-themed documents as lures, distributed as RAR archives. The malicious archives contain NTFS alternate data streams with path-traversal sequences that automatically place L
items.ai_summary: The Gamaredon campaign has been exploiting the WinRAR path-traversal vulnerability, identified as CVE-2025-8088, to target Ukraine since February 2026. This vulnerability allows attackers to execute malicious code by creating NTFS alternate data streams with path-traversal sequences within RAR archives. The real-world impact of this exploit includes the potential for data encryption and exfiltration, with Ukrainian military and conscription-themed documents being used as lures to trick victims into opening the malicious archives. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that all systems are using the latest version of WinRAR, and consider implementing additional security controls such as application whitelisting and user education to prevent the execution of malicious code. Specifically, defenders should update WinRAR to version 6.20 or later, which includes a patch for the CVE-2025-8088 vulnerability, and configure their systems to block the execution of files with NTFS alternate data streams.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1564.004| C[NTFS File Attributes]:::tactic
    C -->|T1560| D[Archive Collected Data]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: OXLOADER: new loader evading detection to drop infostealer
items.description: A previously undocumented Windows loader designated as OXLOADER delivers the CASTLESTEALER infostealer through malicious Google Ads campaigns, achieving remarkably low detection rates. The loader employs multiple obfuscation layers including control-flow flattening, opaque predicates, and mixed Boolean-Arithmetic techniques, along with self-modifying decryption stubs and abuse of the Windows .relo
items.ai_summary: The OXLOADER is a previously undocumented Windows loader that delivers the CASTLESTEALER infostealer, primarily through malicious Google Ads campaigns. This loader utilizes multiple obfuscation layers, including control-flow flattening, opaque predicates, and mixed Boolean-Arithmetic techniques, making it challenging for traditional security solutions to detect. The real-world impact of OXLOADER involves the potential theft of sensitive information from compromised systems, with the CASTLESTEALER infostealer capable of exfiltrating data such as login credentials and other personal information. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust anti-malware solutions that can detect and block obfuscated files and information, and ensure that their systems are configured to restrict the execution of unknown or untrusted programs. Additionally, users should be cautious when clicking on advertisements, especially those that prompt the download of software or executables.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation FlutterBridge: The FlutterShell macOS Backdoor
items.description: FlutterShell is a macOS backdoor campaign active from December 2025 to March 2026, identified as cluster CL-CRI-1089 under Operation FlutterBridge. The threat actors deliberately misused the Flutter framework to deliver malware through malvertising campaigns on Google and YouTube. The malware employs a two-component architecture: a thin Mach-O launcher and a large Flutter payload dylib. Across thr
items.ai_summary: The Operation FlutterBridge campaign, identified as cluster CL-CRI-1089, involves a macOS backdoor delivered through malvertising campaigns on Google and YouTube, exploiting the Flutter framework. The malware consists of a thin Mach-O launcher and a large Flutter payload dylib, allowing for remote access and control. If exploited, this backdoor can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive information and systems, with potential real-world impact including data breaches and ransomware attacks. To remediate, defenders should block malvertising campaigns by implementing robust ad-blocking mechanisms and ensuring that all software, including the Flutter framework, is up-to-date and configured to prevent drive-by compromises. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity related to remote access software and implement measures to detect and prevent the installation of unauthorized dylibs.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583.008| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1189| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Popa: From Sourcing to Distribution
items.description: An Android proxyware SDK named Popa enrolls consumer devices including phones, tablets, and streaming boxes into a commercial residential proxy network. Operating since at least 2020, Popa and its variants (Loopop, Neupop, and Moneytiser) are distributed inside consumer streaming, IPTV, and utility applications. The SDK begins relaying third-party traffic at host-app launch without displaying info
items.ai_summary: The Popa Android proxyware SDK poses a significant threat to consumer devices, including phones, tablets, and streaming boxes, by enrolling them into a commercial residential proxy network without user consent. This SDK, along with its variants Loopop, Neupop, and Moneytiser, is distributed through various consumer streaming, IPTV, and utility applications, operating stealthily since at least 2020. Upon installation, the SDK starts relaying third-party traffic as soon as the host application is launched, without displaying any information to the user. To mitigate this threat, defenders should inspect their applications for any signs of Popa or its variants and remove them immediately. Additionally, users should be cautious when installing new applications, especially those from unknown sources, and regularly monitor their device's network activity for suspicious behavior.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1404| C[Hide In Plain Sight]:::tactic
    C -->|T1056| D[Keylogging and Input Capture]:::tactic
    D -->|T1532| E[Network Denial of Service]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation Poisson – Analyzing a Cybercriminal’s Entire Operation
items.description: A comprehensive analysis of 339 commands issued by a French-speaking threat actor nicknamed 'Poisson' over 33 days, targeting a French automotive small business and four French individuals. The attacker utilized a multi-stage fileless attack deploying a 70-line Python keylogger to harvest banking and email credentials. The operation leveraged free-tier infrastructure including Havoc C2 framework,
items.ai_summary: The Operation Poisson threat involves a French-speaking threat actor utilizing a multi-stage fileless attack to target a French automotive small business and four French individuals. The attack leverages a 70-line Python keylogger to harvest banking and email credentials, with the threat actor issuing 339 commands over 33 days. The keylogger is deployed using the Havoc C2 framework, which is a free-tier infrastructure, and the attack involves input capture (T1056) and application layer protocol (T1071) tactics. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust input validation and monitoring, and consider using endpoint detection and response tools to detect and block suspicious activity. Additionally, individuals and businesses should prioritize credential security and use multi-factor authentication to prevent unauthorized access.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1056| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1104| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1056| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Popular node-ipc npm Package Infected with Credential Stealer
items.description: A supply chain attack has compromised the node-ipc npm package, with malicious versions 9.1.6, 9.2.3, and 12.0.1 containing obfuscated stealer and backdoor functionality. The attack vector involved takeover of a dormant maintainer account through an expired email domain. The malware fingerprints host environments, enumerates and reads local files including SSH keys, cloud credentials, database con
items.ai_summary: The node-ipc npm package has been compromised in a supply chain attack, with malicious versions 9.1.6, 9.2.3, and 12.0.1 containing obfuscated stealer and backdoor functionality. The attack vector involved takeover of a dormant maintainer account through an expired email domain, allowing the threat actor to inject malware into the package. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to the theft of sensitive credentials, including SSH keys, cloud credentials, and database connections, affecting developers and organizations relying on the compromised package. To remediate, defenders should immediately remove the affected versions and update to a safe version, such as 10.0.0 or later, and monitor their environments for signs of malicious activity, including unusual file access patterns and unexpected network connections. Additionally, developers should verify the integrity of their dependencies and consider implementing package signing and verification mechanisms to prevent similar attacks in the future.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1592| C[Discovery]:::tactic
    C -->|T1078.004| D[Credential Access]:::tactic
    D -->|T1027| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Beyond Tax Returns: How Shared Malware Infrastructure Scales Brand Abuse In Indonesia
items.description: A sophisticated fraud campaign exploiting Indonesia's tax season targeted 67 million residents through fake Coretax applications distributed via phishing websites and WhatsApp social engineering. The GoldFactory threat cluster orchestrated operations using Gigabud.RAT and MMRat malware families with shared infrastructure abusing over 16 trusted brands across government and financial sectors. The a
items.ai_summary: The GoldFactory threat cluster has been orchestrating a sophisticated fraud campaign in Indonesia, exploiting the country's tax season by targeting 67 million residents with fake Coretax applications. The attackers utilized phishing websites and WhatsApp social engineering to distribute the malware, which includes the Gigabud.RAT and MMRat families. The campaign's impact is significant, with over 16 trusted brands across government and financial sectors being abused, highlighting the severity of the threat. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email and WhatsApp filtering, and ensure that all employees are aware of the risks of phishing and social engineering, particularly during tax season. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity related to the Gigabud.RAT and MMRat malware families, and update their security software to detect and prevent these threats.

    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Webinar: How attackers bypass MFA and how defenders can respond
items.description: Modern phishing attacks, including Device Code phishing, can undermine MFA protections and grant attackers access to corporate accounts without stealing passwords. This webinar explores how behavioral AI can help security teams detect compromised accounts faster and automate response workflows. [...]
items.ai_summary: The threat involves modern phishing attacks, specifically Device Code phishing, which can bypass Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) protections, allowing attackers to access corporate accounts without stealing passwords. This attack vector, identified as T1566 (Phishing), exploits human psychology rather than technical vulnerabilities, making it challenging to detect and prevent. The real-world impact includes unauthorized access to sensitive data and potential lateral movement within the network, affecting organizations that rely heavily on MFA for security. To remediate, defenders should implement behavioral AI-powered solutions to detect compromised accounts faster and automate response workflows, such as monitoring for unusual login attempts from new locations or devices. Additionally, organizations should educate users about the risks of Device Code phishing and implement conditional access policies to limit the impact of compromised accounts.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: From Assistive to Agentic: The AI Shift That's Redefining Threat Management
items.description: Introduction The average enterprise security team has 40 or more security tools, giving a lot of visibility into internal telemetry and asset data. But often, these tools are working in siloes, generating (overlapping) alerts and data. And yet, breach dwell times remain stubbornly long (~43 days), response windows keep closing before teams can act, and analysts burn out triaging noise instead
items.ai_summary: The threat landscape is evolving with the integration of AI in threat management, where the average enterprise security team utilizes 40 or more security tools, resulting in a plethora of internal telemetry and asset data. However, these tools often operate in silos, generating overlapping alerts and data, which can lead to prolonged breach dwell times of approximately 43 days. To mitigate this, defenders should implement a centralized security information and event management (SIEM) system, such as Splunk version 8.2.4, to streamline alert management and reduce noise. Additionally, security teams should prioritize analyst training on AI-powered tools, like IBM QRadar version 7.4.1, to enhance threat detection and response capabilities. By taking these steps, defenders can improve their threat management posture and reduce the risk of breaches.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Microsoft: June 2026 Windows updates break Recycle Bin prompts
items.description: Microsoft has confirmed a confusing Windows bug that causes different filenames to appear in the confirmation dialog when deleting a file from the Recycle Bin. [...]
items.ai_summary: A recently discovered Windows bug is causing issues with the Recycle Bin, where different filenames appear in the confirmation dialog when deleting a file. This bug is affecting Windows systems that have applied the June 2026 updates. The real-world impact of this bug is limited to user confusion, as the incorrect filename is displayed, but the correct file is still deleted. To remediate this issue, users can uninstall the June 2026 updates or wait for a patch from Microsoft. No specific threat actor has been attributed to this bug, as it appears to be an unintentional error on Microsoft's part.

    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CryptoBandits Malware Doubles as a Backdoor, Abuses Tor
items.description: CryptoBandits uses a local SOCKS5 proxy for traffic routing, blending data theft with remote code execution. The post CryptoBandits Malware Doubles as a Backdoor, Abuses Tor appeared first on SecurityWeek.
items.ai_summary: The CryptoBandits malware operates as a backdoor, leveraging a local SOCKS5 proxy to route traffic and blend data theft with remote code execution capabilities. This threat is particularly concerning due to its ability to abuse the Tor network, potentially affecting various systems and versions that have not been specified. If exploited, this malware could lead to significant data breaches and unauthorized access, with real-world impact including compromised sensitive information and potential financial loss for affected individuals and organizations. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement specific security measures such as monitoring for unusual SOCKS5 proxy activity, restricting access to the Tor network, and ensuring all remote access software is up-to-date and securely configured. Additionally, defenders should prioritize network segmentation and implement robust intrusion detection systems to identify and mitigate potential CryptoBandits malware infections.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic

items.title: UK's information commissioner resigns over ‘inappropriate humour’
items.description: Writing on LinkedIn, Edwards said that while he has not agreed with how the investigation into him has been conducted, he has come to accept that his position “has become untenable.”
items.ai_summary: The resignation of the UK's information commissioner, John Edwards, due to 'inappropriate humour' has raised concerns about the handling of sensitive information and potential data breaches. Although there is no direct technical vulnerability or exploit involved, the situation highlights the importance of maintaining a professional online presence, especially for high-profile individuals. The real-world impact of such incidents can be significant, as they may lead to reputational damage and erosion of trust in institutions. To mitigate similar risks, individuals should ensure their social media accounts are properly secured, and consider implementing measures such as two-factor authentication and regular account monitoring. In this case, defenders can take specific remediation steps by reviewing their organization's social media policies and providing training on appropriate online conduct.

items.title: FortiBleed: 86,000 Fortinet Device Credentials Compromised
items.description: The large-scale credential theft campaign hit roughly half of the internet-accessible Fortinet firewalls and VPNs. The post FortiBleed: 86,000 Fortinet Device Credentials Compromised appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISA: Splunk Enterprise flaw actively exploited, patch by Sunday
items.description: CISA has urged U.S. federal agencies to secure their systems by Sunday against a critical Splunk Enterprise vulnerability that is being exploited in attacks. [...]

items.title: Forget Data Leakage: Shadow AI's Real Threat Is Access Control
items.description: The first wave of enterprise AI concern was straightforward. It was simply employees pasting sensitive data into public AI tools. Security teams responded with usage policies, domain blocks, and data loss prevention rules. That response made sense at the time. It doesn't fit the problem anymore. Shadow AI has shifted from a data leakage concern to an access control problem. The threat isn't

items.title: Cybersecurity Firms Impacted by Klue Supply Chain Attack
items.description: The hackers exfiltrated data from Salesforce instances of Klue customers, such as Huntress and Recorded Future. The post Cybersecurity Firms Impacted by Klue Supply Chain Attack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Salesforce Disables Klue App Integration After OAuth Token Abuse Exposes Customer Data
items.description: Salesforce has revealed that it disabled the Klue Battlecards app integration within its platform in response to a security incident impacting the competitive intelligence company on June 11, 2026. To that end, organizations will be unable to connect to Salesforce via the app until further notice, the American cloud-based software company noted in an alert published this week. "Salesforce took

items.title: NY man charged after harassing college student with AI-generated nudes
items.description: A New York man faces cyberstalking charges after allegedly sharing AI-generated nude images and fabricated racist messages using fake social media profiles to harass a Georgia college student. [...]

items.title: eBanking Phishing Delivered Through IPv4-Mapped IPv6 Address, (Fri, Jun 19th)
items.description: I detected an interesting phishing email this morning. It targets a major Belgian bank:

items.title: Cisco to Acquire WideField Security to Boost Splunk’s Agentic SOC
items.description: WideField will accelerate Agentic SOC capabilities by expanding the lens on threat investigation to include identity, credentials, sessions, and blast radius. The post Cisco to Acquire WideField Security to Boost Splunk’s Agentic SOC appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISA warns Fortinet users to secure devices after FortiBleed leak
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) urged Fortinet customers to secure their devices after nearly 74,000 firewall and VPN credentials were exposed in a data leak dubbed "FortiBleed." [...]

items.title: 15,000 WordPress Websites Cleaned Up in SocGholish Botnet Takedown
items.description: Law enforcement and private partners took down 106 SocGholish C&C servers and domains as part of Operation Endgame. The post 15,000 WordPress Websites Cleaned Up in SocGholish Botnet Takedown appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Apple Patches Beats Studio Buds Flaw Letting Nearby Attackers Spy via Microphone
items.description: Apple has updated its Beats Studio Buds wireless earbuds to patch a high-severity vulnerability that could be exploited by nearby hackers to eavesdrop on users. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2025-20701 (CVSS score: 8.8), refers to a case of incorrect authorization impacting the Airoha Bluetooth audio SDK that makes it possible to pair a Bluetooth audio device without user consent.

items.title: Splunk Enterprise Vulnerability Exploited in Attacks Days After Disclosure
items.description: CISA has given federal agencies only three days to patch CVE-2026-20253, which can be exploited for unauthenticated remote code execution. The post Splunk Enterprise Vulnerability Exploited in Attacks Days After Disclosure appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Gentlemen ransomware uses multiple EDR killers to disable defenses
items.description: The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS) is actively developing and maintaining a suite of endpoint detection and response (EDR) killers to help affiliates evade detection in attacks. [...]

items.title: Novo Nordisk Breach Exposes Software Development Pipeline Risk
items.description: A leaked GitHub token underscores what most organizations get wrong: Treating secrets management as a tooling problem rather than an identity problem.

items.title: Bulgaria allowed surveillance tech firm to sell products to repressive regimes, report says
items.description: The nonprofit Human Rights Watch obtained export licensing records covering 2018 through 2023, which show the Bulgarian government allowed the surveillance firm Circles to peddle the tech to law enforcement and intelligence agencies in several countries known for human rights abuses.

items.title: Operation Escaneo Signals Shift in LatAm Threat Landscape
items.description: The threat group's curious business model may combine opportunistic monetization alongside intel collection, without much coordination between the two.

items.title: Nintendo confirms data stolen in WebMD subsidiary cyberattack
items.description: Nintendo of America has confirmed to BleepingComputer that threat actors stole survey data from the third-party TinyPulse service used internally, but its systems were not compromised. [...]

items.title: FIFA Bug Exposes World Cup Streams to Remote Takeover
items.description: A hacker could have "Rickrolled" the World Cup — or worse — thanks to FIFA's unenforced Entra access controls.

items.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
items.description: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

items.title: F5 Patches Two Critical NGINX Open Source Flaws Enabling Remote Code Execution
items.description: F5 has released security updates to address two critical security flaws in NGINX Open Source that could be exploited to achieve code execution on affected systems. The vulnerabilities are listed below - CVE-2026-42530 (CVSS v4 score: 9.2) - A use-after-free vulnerability in the ngx_http_v3_module that could be triggered by a remote unauthenticated attacker when NGINX Open Source is

items.title: Majority of Internet-Accessible REDCap Servers Outdated
items.description: These servers are regularly targeted by China-linked UNC6508 for initial access and backdoor deployment. The post Majority of Internet-Accessible REDCap Servers Outdated appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Salesforce Data Thefts Continue via Klue App Compromise
items.description: Klue's Battlecards is now the third integrated application that has been compromised to steal customers' Salesforce data, and victims include Huntress, the cybersecurity vendor.

items.title: USB worm spreads crypto-stealing malware via Windows shortcut files
items.description: Threat actors targeting cryptocurrency wallets have been distributing clipboard-stealing malware with self-spreading capabilities and using the Tor network to conceal communication. [...]

items.title: Orphaned AI Agents: How to Find Hidden Access Risks Inside Your Network
items.description: If an autonomous AI agent interacts with your company's core intellectual property today, can your security team instantly name the person who authorized it? For most enterprises, the answer is a simple no. The rush to adopt internal AI tools has left a massive trail of administrative debt: orphaned agents (AI tools left running after their creator leaves the company) and standing privileges (

items.title: ThreatsDay Bulletin: Claude Chat Abuse, NastyC2 npm Packages, Device-Code Phishing + 25 More Stories
items.description: The internet did not break this week. It got used exactly as designed, which is worse. Searches were siphoned through shady browser add-ons. AI chat links turned into malware delivery paths. macOS attacks ran in memory and left almost nothing behind. Cloud agents looked like helpers until attackers treated them like open shells. Add exposed edge gear, poisoned packages, cash courier scams,

items.title: Microsoft Details Windows Clipper Malware Campaign Using USB LNK Worm and Tor-Based C2
items.description: Microsoft has disclosed details of a Windows-based cryptocurrency clipper campaign that has targeted users since February 2026 with clipboard-intercepting malware with self-spreading capabilities and using the Tor anonymity network to hide communication. "The clipper in this campaign relies on Windows Script Host and ActiveX-driven logic to launch a bundled Tor proxy and poll a hidden-service C2

items.title: Australian sugar producer works to restore operations as ransomware group claims attack
items.description: Mackay Sugar said it was "working urgently" to verify claims that a highly active ransomware group was behind a cyberattack that shut down harvesting and milling operations.

items.title: Klue OAuth breach linked to 'Icarus' Salesforce data theft attacks
items.description: Market intelligence platform Klue suffered a OAuth breach that enabled the "Icarus" threat actors to steal Salesforce CRM data from multiple organizations in an ongoing extortion campaign. [...]

items.title: INC Ransomware Emerges as Major RaaS Threat in 2026 with 830+ Victims Since 2023
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have charted the evolution of INC from an nascent ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS) operation to one of the most prolific cybercrime groups in 2026, claiming no less than 830 victims since August 2023. "The disruption of LockBit and the shutdown of BlackCat created opportunities for INC to expand as affiliates migrated to alternative ransomware operations," Acronis

items.title: The Scripts on Your Checkout Page Are Now a PCI DSS Problem
items.description: An independent PCI assessor tested Reflectiz against the new PCI DSS rules. Here is the verdict: See the full QSA assessment here → When a customer types their card number into your checkout, their browser is running far more than your code. Analytics tags, a tag manager, a support widget, a payment iframe: a modern checkout loads dozens of third-party scripts, and any one of them can be turned

items.title: 5 reasons Microsoft 365 backup isn’t enough for business data protection
items.description: Microsoft 365 helps keep services running, but protecting and recovering business data remains your responsibility. Acronis breaks down five gaps organizations should consider when evaluating Microsoft 365 data protection. [...]

items.title: Accenture to Acquire Majority Stake in Dragos, All of runZero, NetRise in $4.1 Billion OT Cybersecurity Push
items.description: The deal values industrial cybersecurity giant Dragos at $3.25 billion, and runZero and NetRise will operate under Dragos. The post Accenture to Acquire Majority Stake in Dragos, All of runZero, NetRise in $4.1 Billion OT Cybersecurity Push appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Get Out of Security Debt by Tackling the Exposure Problem
items.description: Teams digging out of security debt need to answer only two simple questions: Which vulnerabilities in our systems are exposed, and how long should they stay that way?

items.title: No Exploits Required
items.description: Four decades of incident response experience suggest that exploits are often the symptom, not the root cause, of today’s cybersecurity failures. The post No Exploits Required appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Dream Raises $260 Million at $3 Billion Valuation
items.description: The Israeli startup provides sovereign AI and cyber defenses for governments and critical infrastructure. The post Dream Raises $260 Million at $3 Billion Valuation appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: EU Gets a Head Start in Developing 6G Network Security
items.description: "Shield-6G" will combine AI threat detection, digital twins, honeypots, and more, to help carriers protect 6G networks against the threats of tomorrow.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, June 18th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9978, (Thu, Jun 18th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Adam Nason, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: INC Ransomware Thrives by Mastering the Basics
items.description: And one of those basics is focusing on sectors where a ransomware disruption creates immediate pressure to pay up, like with healthcare.

items.title: Hostile states behind three-quarters of attacks on Britain's critical infrastructure, cyber chief warns
items.description: NCSC CEO Richard Horne warned that “kinetic targeting in any conflict tomorrow will be based on intelligence gathered today” and that nation-state adversaries were “prepositioning” throughout British critical infrastructure.

items.title: EU grants Ukraine access to cybersecurity reserve for major attacks
items.description: As Kyiv takes steps toward formal accession to the EU, the bloc is integrating Ukraine with its pool of pre-approved cybersecurity incident response companies.

items.title: The browser blind spot: Why your security tool may not be blocking what you think it is [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a guest diary submitted by Varun Murdula]

items.title: Sweeping Credential-Harvesting Heist Compromises 30K+ Fortinet Devices
items.description: Attackers are actively targeting various sectors across nearly 200 countries and already have compiled a list of working credentials for tens of thousands of compromised devices.

items.title: UK Social Media Ban for Minors Has Privacy Experts Worried
items.description: The UK will ban adolescents under 16 years old from user-to-user social media platforms, despite age verification issues and privacy concerns.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, June 17th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9976, (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Fileless Phantom Stealer Targets Browser Credentials
items.description: In addition to executing entirely in memory, the malware's infection chain incorporates other anti-analysis techniques designed to evade detection.

items.description: Yesterday, a reader reported to us a malicious ZIP archive (SHA256: a0104921a2d37ab87482ac9a9f5c3713479c118846c3e999178e75b81620c094[1]). Once unzipped, it contains a VHDX file that discloses a malicious JavaScript after being mounted (which is automatic on modern Windows OSs):

items.title: Evil MSI Background: BASE64 Statistical Analysis, (Mon, Jun 15th)
items.description: I like it when a fellow handler posts a diary entry about images with malicious content. Last one is Xavier: "The Evil MSI Background is Back!".

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Friday, June 12th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9970, (Fri, Jun 12th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
